official_title: Protection of life and personal liberty
official_text: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
simple_explanation: No person — citizen or non-citizen — can be deprived of their life or personal liberty unless the State follows a fair, just, and reasonable procedure established by law. Over time, the Supreme Court has expansively interpreted 'life' to include the right to livelihood, dignity, privacy, clean environment, speedy trial, shelter, and more. This is the most important and most litigated Fundamental Right.
key_points: The most expansively interpreted Fundamental Right in India.
Applies to ALL persons — citizens and non-citizens.
'Life' includes: dignity, livelihood, privacy, clean environment, shelter, education, health, speedy trial, and more.
'Procedure established by law' must be fair, just, and reasonable (Maneka Gandhi case, 1978).
Cannot be suspended even during a National Emergency (44th Amendment).
Right to privacy was read into Article 21 (K.S. Puttaswamy case, 2017).
example: The government cannot demolish someone's house (their livelihood and shelter) without giving them notice and a hearing — this would violate Article 21. Similarly, a prisoner has the right to be treated with dignity and cannot be tortured.
do: Safeguard the life, dignity, and personal liberty of every person.
Ensure that any deprivation of liberty follows fair and just legal procedures.
Understand that 'life' under Article 21 means much more than mere physical existence.
dont: Deprive anyone of liberty without due process of law.
Assume Article 21 only protects against killing — it covers dignity, privacy, and livelihood too.
Ignore this right — it cannot be suspended even during emergencies.

official_title: Right to education
official_text: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine.
simple_explanation: Every child aged 6 to 14 years has a Fundamental Right to free and compulsory education. This was added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment in 2002 and is implemented through the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 (RTE Act). The State is obligated to provide this education, and parents/guardians have a duty to send their children to school.
key_points: Added by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002.
Makes education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14.
Implemented through the RTE Act, 2009.
Private schools must reserve 25% seats for economically weaker sections under RTE.
The corresponding Fundamental Duty was added under Article 51A(k).
Prior to this amendment, right to education was interpreted as part of Article 21.
example: A government school cannot refuse admission to a child aged 8 years. Similarly, private schools with 25% reservation under RTE must admit children from disadvantaged backgrounds without charging fees.
do: Ensure every child between 6 and 14 years receives free education.
Support the RTE Act's provisions including the 25% reservation in private schools.
Report denial of education to children to the appropriate authorities.
dont: Deny a child admission based on economic status or background.
Charge fees to children who qualify under the RTE Act's reserved seats.
Assume this right extends beyond age 14 — it currently covers only 6 to 14 years.

official_title: Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases
official_text: (1) No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice. (2) Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate and no such person shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a magistrate.
simple_explanation: When someone is arrested, they must be: (1) told the reason for arrest immediately, (2) allowed to consult a lawyer of their choice, and (3) produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours. The 24-hour limit does not include travel time. However, these protections do NOT apply to enemy aliens or persons detained under preventive detention laws (like NSA).
key_points: Three rights upon arrest: (1) Right to know grounds, (2) Right to a lawyer, (3) Right to be produced before magistrate within 24 hours.
The 24-hour window excludes travel time from place of arrest to court.
These protections do NOT apply to enemy aliens.
Different rules apply for preventive detention (clauses 4–7).
Preventive detention can be for a maximum of 3 months unless an Advisory Board approves extension.
Parliament can prescribe longer periods of preventive detention by law.
example: If police arrest someone at 2 PM on Monday, they must be brought before a magistrate by 2 PM Tuesday (plus travel time). If not, the detention is illegal. The arrested person can immediately call their lawyer.
do: Know your arrest rights: grounds of arrest, access to lawyer, 24-hour magistrate production.
Assert these rights if you or someone you know is arrested.
Understand that preventive detention has different (more relaxed) rules.
dont: Allow police to hold someone beyond 24 hours without magistrate authorization.
Assume these protections cover enemy aliens or preventive detention cases.
Waive your right to a lawyer upon arrest.

official_title: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
official_text: (1) Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law. (2) Nothing in this article shall prevent the State from imposing compulsory service for public purposes, and in imposing such service the State shall not make any discrimination on grounds only of religion, race, caste or class or any of them.
simple_explanation: Human trafficking, begar (forced unpaid labour), and all forms of forced labour are completely prohibited and punishable by law. However, the State can impose compulsory service for public purposes (like military conscription or community service) as long as it does not discriminate on grounds of religion, race, caste, or class. This right is enforceable against both the State and private individuals.
key_points: Prohibits human trafficking and all forms of forced labour including begar.
Violation is a punishable criminal offence.
Enforceable against both State and private individuals (horizontal application).
Exception: State can impose compulsory service for public purposes (non-discriminatory).
Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976 enforces this article.
'Begar' means forced labour without payment — a historical practice abolished here.
example: A brick kiln owner forcing workers to work without pay to 'repay' a debt is practicing bonded labour — this violates Article 23 and is punishable under the Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act.
do: Report any instances of forced labour, bonded labour, or human trafficking.
Know that this right protects you against private employers too, not just the government.
Support rehabilitation of victims of trafficking and bonded labour.
dont: Force anyone to work without fair wages or against their will.
Assume that 'debt repayment' through labour is legal — bonded labour is abolished.
Remain silent if you witness trafficking or forced labour.

official_title: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
official_text: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
simple_explanation: Children below 14 years cannot be employed in factories, mines, or any hazardous work. This constitutional prohibition is enforced through the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986 (amended in 2016) and the Factories Act, 1948. The 2016 amendment extended the ban to all forms of child labour (not just hazardous) for children below 14.
key_points: Absolute prohibition of employing children below 14 in factories, mines, and hazardous work.
Enforced through the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986.
The 2016 amendment prohibited child labour in ALL occupations for children below 14.
Adolescents (14–18) are prohibited from hazardous occupations.
Read together with Article 21A (right to education) and Article 39(e) and (f) (directive principles).
India ratified ILO Conventions on child labour in 2017.
example: Employing a 12-year-old child in a fireworks factory or a mining operation is a criminal offence. Even employing them as domestic help is prohibited under the 2016 amendment to the Child Labour Act.
do: Report instances of child labour to the labour department or police.
Support children's access to education instead of employment.
Understand that the law prohibits ALL employment for children below 14.
dont: Employ or allow the employment of children below 14 in any capacity.
Justify child labour as 'training' or 'helping' — it is illegal.
Employ adolescents (14–18) in hazardous occupations.

official_title: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion
official_text: (1) Subject to public order, morality and health and to the other provisions of this Part, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion. (2) Nothing in this article shall affect the operation of any existing law or prevent the State from making any law— (a) regulating or restricting any economic, financial, political or other secular activity which may be associated with religious practice; (b) providing for social welfare and reform or the throwing open of Hindu religious institutions of a public character to all classes and sections of Hindus.
simple_explanation: Every person has the right to: (1) freedom of conscience — to believe or not believe in any religion, (2) profess — to declare their faith openly, (3) practice — to perform religious rituals and ceremonies, and (4) propagate — to spread their religion. This right is subject to public order, morality, and health. The State can regulate secular activities associated with religion and can reform religious practices.
key_points: Four dimensions: conscience, profession, practice, and propagation of religion.
Available to ALL persons — citizens and non-citizens.
Subject to public order, morality, and health.
The State can regulate economic/secular activities associated with religion.
The State can legislate for social reform of Hindu religious institutions.
'Propagation' means the right to spread religion, but does NOT include a right to convert through force or fraud.
example: A person can choose to follow Buddhism, publicly declare their faith, practice Buddhist meditation and rituals, and share Buddhist teachings with others. However, forcible conversion through intimidation or fraud is not protected.
do: Respect everyone's right to their own religious beliefs and practices.
Exercise your religious freedom within the bounds of public order and morality.
Understand the difference between propagation (sharing beliefs) and forced conversion.
dont: Force or coerce anyone to convert to a particular religion.
Use religious freedom to justify practices harmful to public health or morality.
Discriminate against anyone based on their religion or lack thereof.

official_title: Freedom to manage religious affairs
official_text: Subject to public order, morality and health, every religious denomination or any section thereof shall have the right— (a) to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes; (b) to manage its own affairs in matters of religion; (c) to own and acquire movable and immovable property; and (d) to administer such property in accordance with law.
simple_explanation: Every religious group (denomination) has the right to: (1) establish religious and charitable institutions, (2) manage its own religious affairs, (3) own and acquire property, and (4) administer that property according to law. These rights are subject to public order, morality, and health. This protects the organizational autonomy of religious bodies.
key_points: Protects the collective rights of religious denominations (groups).
Four specific rights: establish institutions, manage religious affairs, own property, administer property.
Subject to public order, morality, and health.
The State can regulate the secular (non-religious) aspects of religious institutions.
Courts distinguish between 'essential religious practices' (protected) and secular activities (regulable).
Administration of property must be 'in accordance with law.'
example: A church, mosque, or temple can establish schools and hospitals, manage its own worship practices, and own land. However, the State can require financial audits of temple properties or regulate how charitable funds are spent.
do: Respect the autonomy of religious organizations in managing their religious affairs.
Understand that the State can regulate secular activities of religious institutions.
Ensure religious property is administered transparently and according to law.
dont: Assume religious organizations are exempt from all state regulation.
Interfere with the essential religious practices of any denomination.
Misappropriate or mismanage religious property.

official_title: Freedom as to payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion
official_text: No person shall be compelled to pay any taxes, the proceeds of which are specifically appropriated in payment of expenses for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion or religious denomination.
simple_explanation: No person can be forced to pay tax if the tax money is specifically used to promote or maintain a particular religion. This ensures that public funds collected through taxes are not used for religious purposes. However, this does not prohibit the State from spending general revenue on religious institutions for secular purposes (like maintenance of historical temples).
key_points: Prohibits compulsory tax collection for religious promotion.
The keyword is 'specifically appropriated' — general taxation spent incidentally on religion is not prohibited.
Supports the secular character of the Indian State.
Does not prevent fees charged by religious institutions to their own members.
The State can spend from general revenue on religious sites for heritage/cultural preservation.
example: The government cannot levy a special tax on all citizens and use the money exclusively to build temples. However, using general budget funds to maintain the Taj Mahal (a historical monument that happens to be a mosque) is permissible.
do: Understand that taxes should not be specifically allocated for religious promotion.
Support the secular use of public funds.
Distinguish between religious promotion and cultural/heritage preservation.
dont: Assume all government spending on religious sites is unconstitutional — context matters.
Confuse voluntary religious donations with compulsory taxation.
Demand that the State fund any particular religious activity through tax money.

official_title: Freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship in certain educational institutions
official_text: (1) No religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds. (2) Nothing in clause (1) shall apply to an educational institution which is administered by the State but has been established under any endowment or trust which requires that religious instruction shall be imparted in such institution. (3) No person attending any educational institution recognised by the State or receiving aid out of State funds shall be required to take part in any religious instruction that may be imparted in such institution or to attend any religious worship that may be conducted in such institution or in any premises attached thereto unless such person or, if such person is a minor, his guardian has given his consent thereto.
simple_explanation: No religious instruction can be given in fully State-funded educational institutions. If an institution is State-administered but was established under a religious endowment/trust, religious instruction is allowed. In State-recognized or State-aided institutions, no student can be forced to attend religious instruction or worship without their consent (or their guardian's consent if minor).
key_points: Complete ban on religious instruction in fully State-funded institutions.
Exception for State-administered institutions founded under religious endowments.
In aided/recognized institutions: religious instruction is allowed but CANNOT be compulsory.
Consent is required — the student (or guardian) must agree to attend.
Reinforces the secular nature of State-funded education.
example: A government school (fully funded by the State) cannot conduct daily prayers of any particular religion during school hours. However, a university established by a religious trust but administered by the State can offer religious classes — but students cannot be forced to attend.
do: Ensure religious instruction in State-aided schools is voluntary, not mandatory.
Respect the right of students to opt out of religious instruction.
Understand the distinction between fully State-funded vs. State-aided institutions.
dont: Force any student to participate in religious instruction or worship.
Impart religious instruction in fully government-funded schools.
Assume all religious education is banned — it's only compulsory religious education that's restricted.

official_title: Protection of interests of minorities
official_text: (1) Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same. (2) No citizen shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the State or receiving aid out of State funds on grounds only of religion, race, caste, language or any of them.
simple_explanation: Every group of citizens with a distinct language, script, or culture has the right to preserve and protect it. Additionally, no citizen can be denied admission to any State-maintained or State-aided educational institution based on religion, race, caste, or language. This article protects both linguistic and cultural minorities.
key_points: Two separate protections: (1) Right to conserve distinct language/script/culture, (2) Non-discrimination in educational admissions.
Article 29(1) applies to ANY section of citizens — not just 'minorities.'
Article 29(2) prohibits religion, race, caste, or language-based discrimination in State educational institutions.
This is different from Article 30, which specifically deals with minorities' right to establish educational institutions.
The right to conserve culture includes the right to preserve language and cultural practices.
example: Tamil-speaking citizens in Karnataka have the right to maintain Tamil schools and cultural organizations. A State university cannot deny admission to a student because they speak a different language.
do: Support the preservation of diverse languages, scripts, and cultures.
Ensure no discrimination in educational admissions based on religion, caste, or language.
Understand this right belongs to any cultural group, not just officially designated minorities.
dont: Deny educational admission based on religion, race, caste, or language.
Suppress or force assimilation of any linguistic or cultural group.
Confuse Article 29 (cultural conservation) with Article 30 (minority educational institutions).

official_title: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions
official_text: (1) All minorities, whether based on religion or language, shall have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice. (1A) In making any law providing for the compulsory acquisition of any property of an educational institution established and administered by a minority, the State shall ensure that the amount fixed by or determined under such law for the acquisition of such property is such as would not restrict or abrogate the right guaranteed under that clause. (2) The State shall not, in granting aid to educational institutions, discriminate against any educational institution on the ground that it is under the management of a minority, whether based on religion or language.
simple_explanation: Religious and linguistic minorities have the right to establish and run their own educational institutions. The State cannot discriminate against minority institutions when granting financial aid. If the State acquires property of a minority institution, it must pay fair compensation that doesn't effectively destroy the institution. This is a key article protecting minority educational rights in India.
key_points: Minorities (religious or linguistic) can establish and administer educational institutions.
'Administer' includes the right to manage, set curriculum (within reason), and make hiring decisions.
The State cannot discriminate in granting aid to minority institutions.
Fair compensation required if State acquires property of minority institutions (added by 44th Amendment).
The T.M.A. Pai Foundation case (2002) was a landmark judgment interpreting this article.
Minority status is determined state-wise, not nationally.
example: The Christian community can establish and manage schools and colleges. The State must treat these institutions equally when distributing education grants — it cannot deny funding solely because the institution is run by a minority community.
do: Respect the right of minorities to establish and run educational institutions.
Ensure equal treatment of minority and non-minority institutions in matters of State aid.
Understand that minority status varies by state — a group may be a minority in one state but not another.
dont: Discriminate against minority educational institutions in funding or recognition.
Assume minority institutions are completely exempt from all regulation — reasonable regulation applies.
Confuse majority community's rights with minority rights under this article.

official_title: Compulsory acquisition of property (Repealed)
official_text: [Repealed by the Constitution (Forty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1978, s. 6 (w.e.f. 20-6-1979)]
simple_explanation: Article 31, which guaranteed the right to property as a Fundamental Right, was repealed by the 44th Constitutional Amendment in 1978. The right to property is now only a constitutional right under Article 300A (not a Fundamental Right). This means the State can acquire private property for public purposes by following due process and paying compensation, but the owner cannot approach the Supreme Court under Article 32 for violation of property rights.
key_points: Originally guaranteed the right to property as a Fundamental Right.
Repealed by the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978.
Right to property is now a constitutional right (Article 300A), not a Fundamental Right.
The government can acquire private property for public purpose with compensation.
Property rights can now be enforced only in High Courts, not the Supreme Court under Article 32.
This was done to enable land reform and redistribution without constitutional challenges.
example: If the government wants to build a highway through your land, it can acquire the land under the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013. You have a right to fair compensation but cannot claim violation of a Fundamental Right.
do: Understand that property rights still exist but as a constitutional right under Article 300A.
Know your rights to fair compensation under the Land Acquisition Act, 2013.
Challenge unfair acquisition in the High Court.
dont: Assume the right to property is still a Fundamental Right — it was downgraded in 1978.
Believe the State can take property without any compensation — Article 300A still requires due process.
File a Supreme Court petition under Article 32 for property rights — use Article 226 (High Court) instead.

official_title: Remedies for enforcement of rights conferred by this Part
official_text: (1) The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed. (2) The Supreme Court shall have power to issue directions or orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari, whichever may be appropriate, for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by this Part. (3) Without prejudice to the powers conferred on the Supreme Court by clauses (1) and (2), Parliament may by law empower any other court to exercise within the local limits of its jurisdiction all or any of the powers exercisable by the Supreme Court under clause (2). (4) The right guaranteed by this article shall not be suspended except as otherwise provided for by this Constitution.
simple_explanation: Article 32 gives every person the right to directly approach the Supreme Court if their Fundamental Rights are violated. The Supreme Court can issue five types of writs: Habeas Corpus (against illegal detention), Mandamus (to compel a public authority to act), Prohibition (to stop inferior courts), Quo Warranto (to challenge someone holding public office), and Certiorari (to quash orders). Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 the 'heart and soul of the Constitution.'
key_points: Guarantees direct access to the Supreme Court for Fundamental Rights violations.
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called it the 'heart and soul of the Constitution.'
Five writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, Certiorari.
Article 32 itself is a Fundamental Right — the right to constitutional remedies.
This right cannot be suspended except during a National Emergency (and even then, only for certain rights).
Article 226 provides similar powers to High Courts — but Article 32 is wider.
example: If the police detain someone illegally, the detained person (or anyone on their behalf) can directly file a Habeas Corpus petition in the Supreme Court under Article 32, and the Court can order immediate release.
do: Know that you can directly approach the Supreme Court if your Fundamental Rights are violated.
Understand the five types of writs and when each applies.
Exercise this right — it is itself a Fundamental Right.
dont: Assume this article can be used for non-Fundamental Rights issues (use Article 226 for broader relief).
Hesitate to approach the Supreme Court for genuine Fundamental Rights violations.
Forget that this right is near-absolute — it can be suspended only under very limited emergency conditions.

official_title: Power of Parliament to modify the rights conferred by this Part in their application to Forces, etc.
official_text: Parliament may, by law, determine to what extent any of the rights conferred by this Part shall, in their application to,— (a) the members of the Armed Forces; or (b) the members of the Forces charged with the maintenance of public order; or (c) persons employed in any bureau or other organisation established by the State for purposes of intelligence or counter intelligence; or (d) person employed in, or in connection with, the telecommunication systems set up for the purposes of any Force, bureau or organisation referred to in clauses (a) to (c), be restricted or abrogated so as to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and the maintenance of discipline among them.
simple_explanation: Parliament can restrict or even take away Fundamental Rights for members of the armed forces, police forces, intelligence agencies, and related personnel. This is to ensure discipline and proper functioning of these critical institutions. Only Parliament — not state legislatures — has this power.
key_points: Parliament can restrict Fundamental Rights for armed forces, police, intelligence personnel.
Purpose: maintaining discipline and ensuring proper discharge of duties.
Only Parliament has this power — state legislatures cannot do this.
Covers armed forces, public order forces, intelligence agencies, and related telecom personnel.
Army Act, Navy Act, and Air Force Act contain such restrictions.
example: A soldier cannot form a trade union (restricting Article 19(1)(c)) because the Army Act, enacted under Article 33, prohibits it to maintain military discipline.
do: Understand that military and police personnel have certain restrictions on their freedoms for discipline.
Know that only Parliament can impose these restrictions — not the executive.
Respect the need for discipline in security forces.
dont: Assume armed forces personnel have zero rights — they retain rights not specifically restricted.
Believe state governments can restrict these rights — only Parliament can.
Use this article to justify arbitrary restrictions beyond what Parliament has enacted.

official_title: Restriction on rights conferred by this Part while martial law is in force in any area
official_text: Notwithstanding anything in the foregoing provisions of this Part, Parliament may by law indemnify any person in the service of the Union or of a State or any other person in respect of any act done by him in connection with the maintenance or restoration of order in any area within the territory of India where martial law was in force or validate any sentence passed, punishment inflicted, forfeiture ordered or other act done under martial law in any such area.
simple_explanation: When martial law is in force in any area, Parliament can pass a law to protect (indemnify) military and other officials from legal action for acts done to maintain order. Parliament can also validate sentences, punishments, and orders made under martial law. This article protects officials acting in good faith during martial law situations.
key_points: Allows Parliament to indemnify officials for acts done during martial law.
Parliament can validate martial law actions retroactively.
The Constitution does not define 'martial law' — it means military rule in an area.
Different from National Emergency (Article 352) — martial law is more extreme and localized.
Only Parliament can provide indemnity — not state legislatures.
This has never been invoked in independent India.
example: If martial law was declared in a region and military officers took actions to restore order (including restricting movement or detaining suspects), Parliament could later pass a law protecting those officers from lawsuits.
do: Understand the difference between martial law (Article 34) and emergency provisions (Articles 352–360).
Know that martial law has never been formally declared in independent India.
Recognize that this is an extreme provision for extraordinary situations.
dont: Confuse martial law with national emergency — they are different concepts.
Assume any executive authority can declare martial law — the Constitution is silent on who can.
Use this article to justify unlimited military power — fundamental principles of justice still apply.

official_title: Legislation to give effect to the provisions of this Part
official_text: Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution,— (a) Parliament shall have, and the Legislature of a State shall not have, power to make laws— (i) with respect to any of the matters which under clause (3) of article 16, clause (3) of article 32, article 33 and article 34 may be provided for by law made by Parliament; and (ii) for prescribing punishment for those acts which are declared to be offences under this Part; and (b) any law in force immediately before the commencement of this Constitution in the territory of India with respect to any of the matters referred to in sub-clause (i) of clause (a) or providing for punishment for those acts which are declared to be offences under this Part and in force immediately before such commencement, shall, subject to the terms thereof and to any adaptations and modifications that may be made therein under article 372, continue in force until altered or repealed or amended by Parliament.
simple_explanation: Only Parliament (not state legislatures) has the power to make laws for: (1) matters specified in Articles 16(3), 32(3), 33, and 34, and (2) prescribing punishments for offences declared under Part III (like untouchability under Article 17). Pre-existing laws on these subjects continue until Parliament changes them.
key_points: Reserves certain legislative powers exclusively to Parliament, not state legislatures.
Covers: residence qualifications for public employment (Art. 16(3)), empowering courts (Art. 32(3)), armed forces restrictions (Art. 33), martial law indemnity (Art. 34).
Only Parliament can prescribe punishment for offences under Part III (e.g., untouchability).
Pre-Constitution laws on these subjects continue until Parliament amends them.
This ensures uniformity in fundamental rights enforcement across India.
example: The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 (punishing untouchability under Article 17) was enacted by Parliament under Article 35. No state legislature can make its own version of this law.
do: Understand that certain fundamental rights laws can only come from Parliament.
Know that this ensures nationwide uniformity in fundamental rights protection.
Recognize that pre-existing laws continue until Parliament acts.
dont: Assume state legislatures can make laws on all fundamental rights matters.
Ignore the importance of parliamentary supremacy in fundamental rights legislation.
Confuse Article 35 with Article 13 — Article 13 deals with validity, Article 35 deals with legislative competence.

official_title: Definition
official_text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' has the same meaning as in Part III.
simple_explanation: Article 36 defines 'the State' for Part IV (Directive Principles of State Policy) to have the same meaning as in Article 12 of Part III. This means 'the State' includes the Central Government, State Governments, Parliament, State Legislatures, local bodies, and other authorities. This definition ensures that Directive Principles apply to all levels of government.
key_points: Defines 'the State' for Part IV (Directive Principles).
Same definition as Article 12 — includes all levels of government.
Ensures Directive Principles are guidelines for all government bodies.
This is a definitional article with no substantive rights or duties.
Part IV begins with this article (Articles 36–51).
example: When Article 39 directs 'the State' to ensure equal pay for equal work, this applies to the Central Government, all State Governments, local bodies like municipalities, and other government authorities.
do: Understand that Directive Principles are meant for all levels of government.
Read Article 36 together with Article 12 for the full definition of 'the State.'
Know that Part IV starts here and runs through Article 51.
dont: Confuse this with a substantive right — it's only a definition.
Narrow the meaning of 'State' to only the Central Government.
Forget that Directive Principles, while not justiciable, are fundamental in governance.

official_title: Application of the principles contained in this Part
official_text: The provisions contained in this Part shall not be enforceable by any court, but the principles therein laid down are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws.
simple_explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV) are NOT enforceable by courts — no one can sue the government for not implementing them. However, they are declared 'fundamental in the governance of the country,' meaning the government should keep them in mind when making laws and policies. Over time, the Supreme Court has held that laws implementing Directive Principles can sometimes override certain Fundamental Rights.
key_points: Directive Principles are NOT justiciable — courts cannot enforce them.
Despite being non-enforceable, they are 'fundamental in governance.'
The State has a duty to apply these principles when making laws.
The Minerva Mills case (1980) held that there must be a balance between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles.
Many Directive Principles have been implemented through legislation (e.g., minimum wages, education).
They serve as a 'conscience of the Constitution' guiding policy direction.
example: A citizen cannot sue the government in court for not providing a living wage (Article 43). However, the government enacted the Minimum Wages Act, 1948, implementing this Directive Principle through legislation.
do: Understand that Directive Principles guide government policy, not individual rights.
Recognize that many important laws are based on Directive Principles.
Support the implementation of Directive Principles through democratic processes.
dont: Try to enforce Directive Principles directly in court — they are non-justiciable.
Dismiss Directive Principles as unimportant — they are fundamental in governance.
Assume Directive Principles are merely aspirational — many have been legislated into binding laws.

official_title: State to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people
official_text: (1) The State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life. (2) The State shall, in particular, strive to minimise the inequalities in income, and endeavour to eliminate inequalities in status, facilities and opportunities, not only amongst individuals but also amongst groups of people residing in different areas or engaged in different vocations.
simple_explanation: The State must work toward creating a society where social, economic, and political justice prevails in all institutions. Specifically, the State should minimize income inequality and eliminate inequalities in status, facilities, and opportunities among people and groups, regardless of where they live or what work they do. This is the foundational directive for a welfare state.
key_points: Directs the State to establish a welfare-oriented social order.
Justice must be social, economic, and political — echoing the Preamble.
The State must minimize income inequality (added by 44th Amendment).
Must eliminate inequalities among individuals AND groups.
Applies across geographical regions and vocational groups.
This is the broadest and most aspirational Directive Principle.
example: Government programs like MGNREGA (providing rural employment), subsidized food through PDS, and progressive taxation are all rooted in the directive of Article 38 to reduce inequality and promote welfare.
do: Support policies that reduce inequality and promote social justice.
Understand that welfare state principles guide India's economic policy.
Recognize that programs like MGNREGA and PDS implement this directive.
dont: Assume the government has no constitutional basis for welfare programs — Article 38 provides it.
Ignore regional and vocational inequalities — the directive covers these explicitly.
Dismiss inequality reduction as political — it's a constitutional directive.

official_title: Certain principles of policy to be followed by the State
official_text: The State shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing— (a) that the citizens, men and women equally, have the right to an adequate means of livelihood; (b) that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good; (c) that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment; (d) that there is equal pay for equal work for both men and women; (e) that the health and strength of workers, men and women, and the tender age of children are not abused and that citizens are not forced by economic necessity to enter avocations unsuited to their age or strength; (f) that children are given opportunities and facilities to develop in a healthy manner and in conditions of freedom and dignity and that childhood and youth are protected against exploitation and against moral and material abandonment.
simple_explanation: The State should ensure: (a) adequate livelihood for all citizens regardless of gender, (b) equitable distribution of resources for the common good, (c) prevention of wealth concentration, (d) equal pay for equal work for men and women, (e) protection of worker health and prevention of child labour, and (f) healthy development of children with dignity and protection from exploitation.
key_points: Six specific policy directions covering livelihood, resources, wealth, wages, workers, and children.
Article 39(a): Right to adequate livelihood — basis for MGNREGA.
Article 39(b) and (c): Prevent concentration of wealth — basis for nationalization and land reform.
Article 39(d): Equal pay for equal work — legislated through the Equal Remuneration Act, 1976.
Article 39(e) and (f): Protect workers and children — basis for child labour laws.
Read with Article 21 — the Supreme Court has linked livelihood to the right to life.
example: The Equal Remuneration Act, 1976, which mandates equal pay for men and women doing the same work, directly implements Article 39(d). MGNREGA implements Article 39(a) by guaranteeing employment.
do: Advocate for equal pay for equal work regardless of gender.
Support policies that prevent exploitation of workers and children.
Understand that equitable resource distribution is a constitutional goal.
dont: Pay women less than men for the same work — this violates Article 39(d)'s spirit and existing law.
Exploit children or force them into unsuitable labour.
Allow concentration of wealth to the detriment of the common good.
